title: Egg on Polyester
timeEstimate: 15–40 minutes; cold-only protein, raw vs cooked fork
stainChemistry: Egg is concentrated protein from the white plus fat from the yolk. The protein cooks fast under heat — even warm tap water will instantly cook a raw-egg stain into the fiber and turn a tractable cleanup into a permanent film. Polyester locks egg in two ways: heat sets the protein into the fiber AND the polyester is OLEOPHILIC, grabbing the yolk fat tight. Raw egg responds to a cool enzyme pretreat plus Dawn. Cooked egg (already heat-set on the fiber) is much harder — the protein is locked, and home recovery is unreliable; pro is often the right call. The recurring trap is reaching for warm water to 'help dissolve' egg — exactly the wrong move.
whyThisWorks: Cool water keeps raw egg protein soluble. An enzyme pretreat dismantles the protein BEFORE Dawn handles the yolk fat. Cool wash plus AIR-DRY locks nothing. The honest cap: cooked or hot-washed egg on polyester is often permanent because the protein has bonded to the fiber the same way a cooked egg bonds to a hot pan.
scienceNote: Mechanism (pro-facing): egg white is concentrated albumin protein; egg yolk adds lipid + lecithin. Albumin denatures and cross-links irreversibly above ~62°C, forming an insoluble protein film. Pro uses cold liquid Protein Spotter (protease pretreat) plus cold NSD; for cooked-on egg, sodium hydrosulfite at controlled dilution may help residual chromophore but the protein lock is mechanical and often plateaus.
defaultAssumption: Raw egg drop on a polyester garment (cooking spatter, breakfast spill, kid mishap), fresh and not yet hot-rinsed or hot-dried. Cooked-on egg or hot-dried = often permanent; pro for valuable garments.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Plastic spoon edge or dull butter knife
spottingProtocol.instruction: Scrape any thick egg off the surface with a plastic spoon edge. Flat-edge work only — don't rub. Don't pour water yet.
Damp white cloth with COOL water. Press, lift, rotate. Cool only — even warm tap water cooks raw egg into the polyester instantly.

spottingProtocol.equipment: OxiClean Versatile / Biz / Tide Plus per label, COOL water
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dissolve enzyme pretreat in COOL water. Apply to spot, keep damp under a cloth, dwell 20–30 min. Enzyme dismantles raw egg protein at cool temperature. Hot water above bath temperature damages enzymes AND cooks the protein into the polyester.
Generous Dawn directly on the spot. Soft toothbrush 5–10 min. Polyester is oleophilic — extended dwell needed. Cool damp-cloth rinse.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Washing machine, cool water, drying rack
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cool machine wash with normal detergent. AIR-DRY only. Inspect dry. NEVER hot dryer until cleared — locks residual yolk fat and yellows polyester.

steps: 3. Enzyme pretreat in COOL water, 20–30 min — BEFORE any heat.
4. Generous Dawn pretreat for yolk fat, cool, 5–10 min.
5. Cool machine wash + AIR-DRY. Inspect dry before any heat.
products: Enzyme pretreat (OxiClean Versatile / Biz / Tide Plus / liquid Protein Spotter)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool throughout — heat cooks egg protein into polyester permanently
warnings: NEVER hot water — even warm tap water cooks raw egg into the fiber
Order matters — enzyme FIRST (protein), Dawn SECOND (fat)
Polyester is oleophilic — extended Dawn dwell needed
Enzymes denature above bath temperature — keep COOL
Cooked-on egg is often permanent — pro for valuable garments

homeSolutions.agent: Egg Cooks. That's the Whole Trap.
homeSolutions.instruction: Egg protein cooks fast under heat. Even warm tap water will cook a raw-egg stain into the polyester instantly and turn a tractable cleanup into a permanent film. Two hard rules: NO hot water (even warm — cool only) and NO hot dryer until cleared. The order also matters: enzyme pretreat first for the protein, Dawn second for the yolk fat.
If there's a thick bit of egg on the surface, scrape it off with a plastic spoon edge or dull butter knife. Flat-edge work only — never rub. Rubbing pushes egg into the fiber surface.
Damp white cloth with COOL water. Press, lift, rotate. Cool only — anything warmer than tap-cold cooks raw egg into the fiber.

Dissolve an enzyme pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Tide Plus) in COOL water per the label. Apply to the spot, keep damp under a cloth, wait 20 to 30 minutes. The enzyme dismantles raw egg protein at cool temperature. Don't be tempted to use warm water 'to dissolve' the egg — heat above bath temperature damages the enzymes AND cooks the protein.
Yolk has fat that polyester grabs onto. Apply a generous amount of Dawn dish soap. Work in with a soft toothbrush for 5 to 10 minutes. Cool damp-cloth rinse.

Cool machine wash with normal detergent. AIR-DRY only. Inspect dry. NEVER hot dryer until cleared — locks residual fat and yellows polyester. If the egg has already been cooked or hot-washed, accept that recovery is partial — pro is the right call for valuable garments.

steps: 1. NO hot water. NO hot dryer until cleared.
4. Enzyme pretreat in COOL water, 20–30 min — protein FIRST.
5. Generous Dawn pretreat, cool, 5–10 min — fat SECOND.
6. Cool machine wash + AIR-DRY. Inspect dry.
products: Enzyme pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, Tide Plus)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if egg has been cooked or hot-washed (often permanent on polyester). Stop on specialty / performance / dry-clean-only polyester (pro). Stop after 2 home rounds without progress.
warnings: NEVER hot water — even warm tap water cooks raw egg
Order matters — enzyme FIRST, Dawn SECOND
Polyester is oleophilic — extended Dawn dwell needed
Enzymes denature above bath temperature — keep cool
Cooked egg is often permanent on polyester

neverDo: Never warm or hot water on raw egg + polyester
Never skip the Dawn step — leaves yolk-fat shadow
Never use enzymes in hot water — destroys enzymes AND sets protein
fiberSensitivities: Polyester is oleophilic — grabs yolk fat tightly
Polyester is heat-sensitive — yellows under heat
Cooked egg on polyester is often mechanically permanent
Performance / specialty polyester: pro — coatings react unpredictably

materialWarnings: Egg cooks at warm temperatures — cool water only
No hot water / no hot dryer — polyester locks egg
Order matters — enzyme FIRST (protein), Dawn SECOND (fat)
Polyester loves yolk fat — extended Dawn dwell needed

escalation.when: Egg has already been cooked or hot-washed (often permanent). Performance / specialty / dry-clean-only polyester. 2 home rounds plateau. Yellow yolk-fat shadow remains after Dawn.
escalation.whatToTell: "Egg on [white / dyed / athletic / specialty] polyester, [raw / cooked / hot-washed], age [fresh / hours / hot-dried suspected], home-treated with [cool enzyme pretreat + Dawn cool + cool wash + air-dry]. No hot, no dryer. [Residual film / yolk shadow / cooked-on suspected]."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner with combination-stain (protein + oil) chemistry

contextQuestions: Raw egg, scrambled, or cooked-on (eggs benedict yolk drop)?
Whole egg, white only, or yolk only?
White, dyed, athletic, or specialty polyester?
Fresh, hours old, or hot-washed already?
Has anyone tried warm or hot water?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Raw egg on polyester is tractable IF caught before any heat. Even warm tap water cooks it. Cooked-on egg is often permanent because the protein bonds to polyester the same way a cooked egg bonds to a hot pan. Yolk fat is the second-stage cleanup — polyester is oleophilic, so extended Dawn dwell is needed to release it.
